title: HTML Elements, Links, and Media
description: In this lesson, you will move beyond basic text and start building real, interactive webpages. You will learn how to connect pages together using links, display images, organize content with lists, present data using tables, and add audio and video. By the end, your webpages will feel more like real websites instead of simple text documents.
learningObjectives: Understand how links connect webpages together
Create both internal and external links using the anchor tag
Display images correctly using the img tag and its attributes
Organize content clearly using ordered and unordered lists
Create simple tables to display structured data
Embed audio and video into a webpage using HTML5

subtopics.title: Understanding Links (The Backbone of the Web)
subtopics.content: The internet works because of links. Without links, every webpage would be isolated and you would not be able to move from one page to another. In HTML, links are created using the tag (called the anchor tag). Basic example: ```html Go to Google ``` What is happening here? - is the tag - href is an attribute that tells the browser where to go - The text inside is what users click Types of links: 1. External link (goes to another website) ```html Visit Example ``` 2. Internal link (goes to another page in your project) ```html About Us ``` 3. Open in a new tab ```html Open Website ``` Beginner tip: Always make your link text meaningful. Avoid writing 'click here'. Instead, say 'Visit our homepage'.
commonMisconceptions: Links are not only for text — you can wrap images inside links.
If href is missing, the link will not work.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Links are like doors in a building. Each door takes you to a different room (webpage).

subtopics.title: Adding Images to Your Webpage
subtopics.content: Images make your webpage more attractive and easier to understand. In HTML, images are added using the tag. Basic example: ```html ``` Important things to know: - The tag does NOT have a closing tag - src = where the image is located - alt = description of the image Why is alt important? - Helps visually impaired users (screen readers) - Shows text if image fails to load - Improves SEO (search engines understand your image) You can also control size: ```html ``` Beginner tip: Always use simple and clear file names like 'logo.png' instead of 'IMG1234.png'.
commonMisconceptions: Images do not display without a correct file path.
alt text is not optional — it is very important.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Images are like pictures in a textbook that help explain the topic better.

subtopics.title: Lists (Making Content Easy to Read)
subtopics.content:
Lists help you organize information so it is easy to read. There are two main types of lists: 1. Unordered list (bullet points) ```html
  • Rice
  • Beans
  • Bread
``` 2. Ordered list (numbered) ```html
  1. Wake up
  2. Brush teeth
  3. Go to school
``` You can also nest lists (list inside a list): ```html
  • Fruits
    • Apple
    • Mango
``` Beginner tip: Use lists whenever you have items — it improves readability.
commonMisconceptions: Lists are not only for decoration — they improve structure.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Lists are like shopping lists or to-do lists that help you stay organized.

subtopics.title: Tables (Displaying Data Clearly)
subtopics.content:
Tables are used when you want to display data in rows and columns. Basic example: ```html
Name Score
John 80
``` Explanation: - table = container - tr = table row - th = heading cell - td = data cell Beginner tip: Use tables only for data, not for designing layouts.
commonMisconceptions: Tables are not meant for page layout.
th is different from td — it represents headings.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Tables are like a school result sheet with rows and columns.

subtopics.title: Adding Audio and Video
subtopics.content:
HTML5 allows you to add audio and video directly without extra software.

Audio example:
```html
<audio controls>
  <source src="song.mp3" type="audio/mpeg" />
</audio>
```

Video example:
```html
<video width="400" controls>
  <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4" />
</video>
```

Important:
- controls adds play/pause buttons
- Always use supported formats (MP3, MP4)

Beginner tip:
Keep media files small so your page loads faster.
commonMisconceptions: Not all browsers support every format.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Adding media is like putting a TV or music player inside your webpage.
